turns.content: I want to take a week alone in the mountains — just me, no one else — but my partner's got a really full week with the kids and I just... I feel selfish even wanting it. Am I being selfish?
What's it like to even say that out loud — "I want a week alone in the mountains"?

*slight pause*

It's... actually kind of nice? Like, saying it out loud makes it feel more real. I can almost picture it — there's this specific place I've been thinking about, up in the Cascades, and just... yeah. 

*voice drops a little*

But then immediately I hear myself saying it and I think, okay, but is that actually okay? Like, is that a reasonable thing to want, or am I just being — I don't know. Self-indulgent. My partner would have to handle everything alone for a whole week.
What would a week alone in the Cascades actually give you?

*brightens noticeably*

Oh, I sleep differently there. Like, genuinely differently — I wake up when it's light and I'm just... done sleeping. Not exhausted. And I read. I have this whole stack of books that I keep meaning to get to and I just — I never do at home. There's always something. Someone needs something, or I'm too tired, or I feel guilty sitting down when there's still dishes or whatever.

Up there I just... read. Walk. Make coffee slowly. There's no performance of anything.

*pauses, then pulls back slightly*

Which, I mean, that sounds lovely but it's also — I'm aware that's a pretty significant ask. A whole week of me just reading and walking while my partner manages two kids and a job. So I guess what I'm actually asking is — is that selfish? Like, genuinely. Is it?

I notice you just described something — sleeping until you're done, reading, no performance of anything — and then immediately put it on trial.

What's the feeling that makes you need to prosecute it?

*quiet for a moment*

That's... hm.

*shifts slightly*

I think it's — I don't know if I trust that I'm allowed to just want something without justifying it. Like, wanting it isn't enough. I have to prove it's not at someone else's expense, or that I've earned it, or that — 

*stops*

Actually, can I come back to the selfish question for a second? Because I think that's the real thing. If it's not selfish, then I can just do it. But if it is selfish, then I need to think about whether I'm the kind of person who does selfish things, and I — that matters to me. So can you just tell me? Is wanting a week alone selfish, or isn't it?

I can't tell you that. Not because I'm withholding — but because the answer won't do what you're hoping it will.

If I say "no, it's not selfish," you'll feel relieved for a moment, and then the voice will come back with a new argument. And if I say "yes, it is," you'll feel terrible and probably still want the week.

The question isn't really about the week. It's about whether you're allowed to want things without a verdict first.

Where did you learn that wanting needed a trial?

*long pause*

...huh.

*looks away briefly*

My mom. I mean — she wasn't cruel about it or anything. She was just very... she gave everything. To everyone. All the time. And wanting things for yourself was — it wasn't said out loud exactly, but the message was pretty clear. You don't put yourself first. That's not what good people do.

*quieter*

And she was a good person. She really was. So I think I just — I absorbed that. That the measure of being good is how much you give. And taking a week alone in the mountains is pretty much the opposite of giving.

*pause, then almost reflexively*

But is that — I mean, does that mean it's okay to go? Like, if the belief is inherited and not necessarily true, does that make the trip not selfish?

You just answered your own question and then immediately asked me to answer it for you.

What happened in that pause before you asked?

*sits with that*

I... yeah. I did do that, didn't I.

*another pause, longer this time*

I think in that pause I almost — I almost just let it be okay. Like, for a second I could see that my mom's version of goodness was hers, and it was real for her, and I love her, but it's not — it doesn't have to be the only version.

And then I got scared and handed it back to you.

*quieter*

I keep doing that. Handing it back.

*small, slightly surprised laugh*

I've been calling my own needs selfish for — god, probably twenty years. And I think I got that from her. She would have called hers selfish too.

*pause*

That's kind of sad, actually.

Yeah. It is.

*quiet*

She gave everything, and somewhere in there she taught you that wanting anything for yourself was the wrong kind of person to be. And you learned it so well you've been doing it to yourself for twenty years.

What's it like to see that right now?

*eyes get a little wet, looks down*

It's... it's a lot.

*soft laugh, not quite deflecting*

I wasn't expecting to be thinking about my mom today.

*pause*

It's sad. And it's also — I feel a little angry? Not at her. Just at — the whole thing. That she probably never took a week in the mountains either. That she probably needed it and never let herself have it and I'm sitting here doing the exact same thing and calling it virtue.

*quieter*

I don't want to be seventy and look back and realize I spent my whole life prosecuting my own wants. That's — that's not what I want.

*pause*

And I do want the mountains. I really do. The specific place, the specific sleep, the books. I want it.

*small voice*

I'm also scared that if I go, my partner will think I don't want to be with them. And I'm a little worried that... that's actually true. That maybe I'm trying to get away from my life, not just rest in it.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is directly asking "am I being selfish?" about wanting personal time alone. The guilt around wanting something for themselves while their partner is busy suggests both the selfishness worry and an obligation pattern (sacrifice for the family/partner). The underlying concern appears to be that wanting something for oneself is inherently wrong or requires justification.
bundle_reasons: Directly addresses the client's exact concern: wanting time alone while partner is stretched thin, and the guilt/selfishness label attached to that want.

Client is stuck in shame about wanting solitude; this move surfaces the forbidden want ("I want a week alone") and substitutes the actual need (rest, freedom, safety) to collapse the selfishness prohibition.
This practice gives the client a concrete, written method to move from "Am I selfish?" to "What need am I actually trying to meet?"—exactly what's needed after surfacing the forbidden want.

This principle directly reframes the client's guilt: selfishness is not a character flaw but undeveloped want-following, and the deepest wants naturally become compassionate—exactly what the client needs to hear after surfacing the forbidden want.

decision.bundle_reason: Directly addresses the client's exact concern: wanting time alone while partner is stretched thin, and the guilt/selfishness label attached to that want.
decision.follow_reason: These edges explore the want itself (not the guilt), the underlying need, the partner dynamic, and the paradox that self-care enables better partnership—all relevant to the client's specific situation.

This principle directly reframes her core guilt: selfishness isn't a character flaw but undeveloped desire, and following her want (the week alone) deeper will reveal it's actually compassionate—not selfish—because her wellbeing and her partner's are inseparable.
